stimulus: A student researching residential rooftop solar power in a mid-sized European city argues that, over the last decade, homeowners and small businesses in the city have steadily moved toward installing physically larger photovoltaic arrays.
stem: Which choice best describes data in the table that support the student's claim?

answerOptions.content: The percentage of new installations larger than 10 kW increased every year between 2012 and 2022.
In 2012, nearly 75% of installations were smaller than 4 kW, whereas only a little more than 20% were in the 4–6 kW range.
No installations in 2012 were larger than 6 kW, whereas the majority of installations in 2022 were larger than 10 kW.
Most installations in 2012 were smaller than 4 kW, whereas most installations in 2022 were 6 kW or larger.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer because it describes data from the table that directly support the student's claim about a shift toward physically larger arrays. The table shows that in 2012, 72% of new installations were less than 4 kW (the smallest category), while in 2022, 34% + 35% = 69% of installations were 6 kW or larger. That contrast (most installations small in 2012; most installations 6 kW or larger in 2022) is precisely a steady move toward larger arrays.

Choice A is incorrect because the table only shows 2012 and 2022, not yearly data; we cannot tell from the table whether the share larger than 10 kW increased every year. Choice B is incorrect because, although the 2012 figures are accurate, the statement says nothing about 2022 and so does not show a shift toward larger arrays over the decade. Choice C is incorrect because the table shows 7% of 2012 installations were in the 6–10 kW range (so installations larger than 6 kW did exist in 2012), and only 35% of 2022 installations were larger than 10 kW—not “the majority.”
